name: Bathtub Mountain
short_description: BLM public rockhounding on northern Idaho's Bathtub Mountain. Find quartz, chalcedony, and agate specimens in this moderate-elevation site accessible year-round with no permits required.
description:
Bathtub Mountain sits at 6,217 feet elevation in the Shoshone County area of northern Idaho, offering rockhounders open BLM access to quality quartz and chalcedony specimens. The site is named for its distinctive rounded topography and sits within the complex geology of the Columbia River Plateau transitional zone, where ancient volcanic activity and subsequent weathering have exposed colorful mineral deposits across moderate slopes covered with mixed conifer forest.

The terrain here reflects Idaho's varied geology: Precambrian quartzite and metamorphic rocks underlie much of Shoshone County, creating excellent conditions for finding well-formed quartz crystals and secondary chalcedony deposits. The moderate slopes mean steady but not extreme elevation gain when hiking to productive collecting areas. Visitors typically work exposed ridgelines and washouts where weathering exposes fresh material.

As BLM land, Bathtub Mountain requires no permits and imposes no fees, making it an accessible option for beginners and experienced rockhounders alike. The 25-pound daily personal use limit applies. Most collectors spend 3-5 hours working hillsides and gullies on foot, scanning for cloudy to translucent quartz chunks and banded chalcedony nodules. The moderate slope means you should expect steady footing and good footwear.

This location attracts rockhounders year-round but is most comfortable June through September when high elevation temperatures moderate and snow clears completely. Winter visits are possible but snow and ice can obscure collecting areas and make hiking treacherous. Spring and fall offer ideal conditions with fewer crowds.
history: Bathtub Mountain's mineral deposits formed during the Precambrian metamorphic events that created much of northern Idaho's quartzite and schist bedrock, later exposed and weathered during the Quaternary. The area sits at the transition between the Columbia River Plateau's flood basalt province to the west and the Idaho batholith to the east, creating diverse mineralogy where quartz veins intruded through metamorphic host rocks and have since been concentrated by erosion.
tips: Focus your search on exposed ridgelines and small gullies where water runoff has weathered away softer matrix rock and concentrated quartz nodules and chalcedony fragments. Bring a rock hammer or pick to test loose material and expose fresh surfaces - many specimens here are partially buried or hidden under lichen. Look for white to milky quartz chunks (fist-sized or smaller) and translucent to banded chalcedony pieces; the best material often sits mixed with rust-stained decomposing host rock near seepage areas.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,217 feet with moderate slopes and mixed conifer forest coverage. Terrain transitions from parking area (estimated 200-400 feet from productive zones) to moderate rocky hillsides with uneven footing. Loose rock and talus fragments are common. Walking distance to best collecting areas is typically 0.25 to 0.75 miles. Sturdy boots required. Some areas may have scattered deadfall.
best_season: June - September. High elevation (over 6,200 feet) means significant winter snow and cold temperatures December through March. Late spring to early fall offers ideal conditions with snow-free slopes, moderate temperatures, and stable weather. Early summer offers the most reliable access but can see higher BLM visitor traffic.
written_directions: From Kellogg (approximately 15 miles south), take Interstate 90 north and exit toward Wallace. From Wallace, head northeast on ID-10 toward the Shoshone County highlands. Bathtub Mountain is accessed via Forest Service roads in the higher elevations northeast of Wallace near the headwaters area. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for the final 3-5 miles on rough dirt roads. Verify current road conditions with Coeur d'Alene BLM office before visiting.
road_conditions: Rough dirt roads the final 3-5 miles to trailhead. High-clearance vehicle strongly recommended. Road may be rough and rutted; impassable during heavy rains or winter.
parking_notes: Small informal pullout area at roadside near trailhead. Capacity approximately 3-4 vehicles. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only loose material and weathered fragments - do not break fresh bedrock. Pack out all waste.
rules:

Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA

No motorized excavation

Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Kellogg and Wallace, Idaho, approximately 15-20 miles south. Both towns offer gas, food, lodging, and water.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky hillsides and loose material. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Moderate elevation gain (600-800 feet over roughly 1 mile) typical for productive areas. Not suitable for limited mobility. Weather exposure at high elevation - bring jacket even in summer.

faq.question: What types of rocks and gems can I find at Bathtub Mountain in Idaho?
faq.answer: Bathtub Mountain is known for quartz, chalcedony, agate, and jasper specimens. The site produces translucent to white quartz chunks ranging from thumbnail to fist size, and banded chalcedony nodules. Most material requires field identification, but experienced rockhounders often find quality specimens suitable for display or cutting.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Bathtub Mountain?
faq.answer: No permit is required at Bathtub Mountain - it is BLM public land open for recreational rockhounding. However, you must follow the 25-pound daily collection limit and personal-use-only rules. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are prohibited.

faq.question: How far from Kellogg is Bathtub Mountain and what vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: Bathtub Mountain is approximately 18 miles north of Kellogg. A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is strongly recommended for the final 3-5 miles of rough dirt road. Road conditions vary seasonally and can be impassable during heavy rain or winter.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Bathtub Mountain?
faq.answer: June through September is ideal. The site sits at 6,217 feet elevation, so significant snow and cold temperatures make winter visits difficult. Early summer offers snow-free slopes and moderate temperatures, though expect more visitor traffic. Early fall provides excellent conditions with fewer crowds.

faq.question: Is Bathtub Mountain family-friendly and can I bring my kids and dog?
faq.answer: Yes, Bathtub Mountain is beginner and family-friendly for kids ages 8 and up. Dogs are welcome on BLM land. The moderate slopes and 0.25- to 0.75-mile walk to productive zones are manageable for most families. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and a jacket - the high elevation exposure means weather can change quickly.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring for rockhounding at Bathtub Mountain?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer or small pick to test loose material and expose fresh surfaces - many specimens here are partially buried. Pack a collection bag, sturdy hiking boots, plenty of water, sun protection, and a jacket even in summer. A hand lens helps identify chalcedony versus other quartz varieties. A GPS unit is helpful given the remote location and spotty cell service.

meta_description: Find quartz, chalcedony, and agate at Bathtub Mountain in Idaho. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit required. Beginner-friendly collecting near Kellogg.
